title: Fwd: RRB-17: rollback loop in private installer (root cause + patch plan)
preview:
From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 13, 2026 at 7:15 AM
Subject: RRB-17: rollback loop in private installer (root cause + patch pla...
text:
From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 13, 2026 at 7:15 AM
Subject: RRB-17: rollback loop in private installer (root cause + patch plan)

Root cause (RRB-17): when CRD migration partially applies, the rollback cleanup hook exits non-zero before clearing `installer-rollback-lock`. The controller treats rollback as failed and schedules another rollback attempt, which sees the lock and repeats. Net effect: "stuck rollback" / infinite rollback retries.

Patch plan:
- In rollback cleanup: clear lock in a `defer`-style best-effort step even if migration revert fails.
- Add lock TTL (annotation with timestamp) and refuse to retry rollback if lock older than N minutes; surface actionable status.
- Add metric `ins...

Hi Samir —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. Per our conversation, I pulled together a compact delta worksheet that maps expected monthly spend, latency/SLO buckets, and an initial staged-credit proposal so your procurement team can see a concrete replacement path. The attached worksheet models three adoption scenarios (Pilot / Ramp / Steady-state) and shows our recommended credit + technical backfill for the migration window.

A couple quick notes:
- We think a tiered temporary credit (higher % in Pilot, step down into Ramp) keeps your unit economics neutral while removing immediate cash outlay.

Ben —

We received a notice from a third party alleging patent infringement tied to outputs generated during a customer proof-of-concept. Our legal team would like to tighten the indemnity and notice language in the MSA/order form before we sign the final PO. Key asks from our side:

- Defense control: we prefer vendor (you) to defend, but only for claims that clearly relate to Redwood's model outputs rather than our data; otherwise we want a carve-out.
- Notice and cure windows: 15 days isn't enough; proposing 30 days for us to respond and supply evidence.
- Evidence preservation: we need a defined evidence request list and contact for triage.
